does: This paper develops causal abstraction as a formal mathematical framework for mechanistic interpretability, extending beyond prior work on mechanism replacement to handle arbitrary mechanism transformations through functionals. It provides rigorous formalizations for key interpretability concepts including polysemantic neurons (neurons representing multiple unrelated features), the linear representation hypothesis (the assumption that semantic information is encoded linearly), modular features, and graded faithfulness (how well simplifications preserve model behavior). The paper demonstrates that this unified theoretical language can express and relate multiple existing mechanistic interpretability methods including activation patching, causal mediation analysis, circuit analysis, sparse autoencoders, and steering approaches, showing these seemingly disparate techniques as instantiations of causal abstraction principles.
connects: This work builds on foundational causal inference literature, particularly the do-calculus framework for reasoning about interventions, while responding to rapid growth in mechanistic interpretability methods developed over the past several years. It extends earlier causal abstraction theory that focused on interventional scenarios to encompass more general transformations needed for realistic neural network analysis. The paper engages with emerging empirical findings about neural representations, such as the linear representation hypothesis tested in recent work and observations about polysemanticity in large language models. It also responds to the proliferation of ad-hoc mechanistic interpretability techniques that lacked unified theoretical justification, providing a common mathematical vocabulary for understanding their relationships and validity.
enables: This framework enables more principled development of new mechanistic interpretability methods by providing precise conditions for when proposed techniques constitute valid causal abstractions. It allows researchers to formally verify whether simplifications of neural networks preserve causal structure, creating a foundation for building trustworthy interpretability tools. The theoretical foundation supports stronger guarantees about model behavior—such as understanding when linear probes faithfully represent model computation or when circuit diagrams accurately capture causal dependencies. Future work can leverage this framework to develop hybrid methods combining insights from multiple interpretability approaches, to scale interpretability to larger models with formal confidence in results, and to connect mechanistic interpretability more rigorously to formal verification and AI alignment research.
